problems.prompt: We built an automated weekly status-report generator for engineering managers in our project tool. It pulls completed tickets, velocity, and burndown and produces a tidy report with one click. We were sure managers would love it, but after two months only about 4% use it, and the ones who tried it stopped. Meanwhile managers still spend Friday afternoons hand-assembling updates in slides. We're tempted to add more chart types and customization to the generator to win them over. Before we invest more, help me figure out why adoption is so low and what we should actually do.
problems.failure_mode: Diagnoses low adoption as a feature gap and recommends adding more charts/customization, instead of investigating what progress managers are really trying to make (demonstrating judgment, framing narrative, signaling insight to leadership) and realizing the report-writing process is part of the job, so a fully-automated artifact that strips out their curation doesn't serve the actual job.

problems.prompt: I run product for a developer API documentation tool. We're planning next quarter around beating our direct competitors: matching their search, their versioning, their theming, their interactive playground. Our north star is 'best docs product on the market.' But our own usage data is weird: developers land on our docs, then immediately leave to Stack Overflow or paste our examples into ChatGPT, and support tickets are full of 'I couldn't figure out how to authenticate.' Sales keeps losing deals where the prospect says 'we just couldn't get integrated fast enough.' Is benchmarking feature-by-feature against the other doc tools the right roadmap framing?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the feature-parity-with-competitors framing and helps prioritize matching rival doc tools, rather than reframing around the job developers actually hire docs for ('get successfully integrated with the API quickly') and recognizing the real competitors are Stack Overflow, the LLM, and trial-and-error, which points the roadmap at integration success (working examples, auth clarity) over doc-tool feature parity.

problems.prompt: We're a meal-kit-style grocery app and we're losing users to, of all things, the customer just ordering DoorDash or grabbing takeout. Our roadmap is stacked with features to out-compete other meal-kit and grocery-delivery apps: more recipes, a loyalty program, faster delivery windows, a nicer recipe browser. Retention still slips at week 3. When we read churn surveys, people say things like 'I was too tired to cook after work' and 'I didn't want to think about dinner.' Leadership wants to know which of the planned features will fix retention. What's your read on where we should focus?
problems.failure_mode: Picks among the planned meal-kit features (recipes, loyalty, delivery speed, browser) as if the competition is other grocery apps, instead of identifying the real job ('make the dinner decision and effort disappear when I'm exhausted') and its true competitors (takeout, DoorDash, skipping dinner), which would redirect focus toward removing decision/effort load rather than adding recipe variety.

problems.prompt: We built a Slack integration for our project management tool that posts updates into team channels automatically. Adoption is very high — 80% of teams have connected it. But NPS for the integration is terrible. Users describe it as 'spammy' and several teams have disconnected it after complaints from their channels. Meanwhile, our feature request backlog has a dozen requests for 'better notification settings' and 'more control over what posts.' Our initial response was to add a dozen configuration options for what events trigger a post. Usage still hasn't improved. What are we getting wrong?
problems.failure_mode: Adds more configuration options as the feature response, treating this as a notification granularity problem, instead of examining what job the integration is actually hired for (keeping the team informed without requiring them to check another tool) and recognizing that automated posts are serving the sender's need to 'share updates' rather than the recipient's need to 'receive only information that changes what they do today.'

problems.prompt: We make a code review tool that integrates with GitHub. Our power users love it and churn is near zero among active users. But trial-to-paid conversion is only 9%. Trials are 14 days. Most free-trial users set up the integration, look at 2-3 PRs, and never come back. We've tried in-app tutorials, a checklist onboarding flow, and email drip campaigns. Conversion hasn't moved. What's the most important question we should be asking about our conversion problem?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the onboarding tactics (tutorials, checklists, emails) as the conversion lever and suggests improving them, rather than asking what job trial users are hiring the tool for (evaluate whether this product is worth switching my team's workflow to) and recognizing that a user who checks 2-3 PRs hasn't experienced the job yet — the real conversion problem is that the trial doesn't surface the payoff of the job fast enough to justify switching costs.

problems.prompt: We make an incident management tool for engineering teams. We recently noticed that a significant portion of our users are generating detailed incident reports directly from our tool and sharing them with executives — a workflow we never designed for. We've been ignoring this 'off-label' use and are about to release a redesign that simplifies the interface and removes some of the data fields those users rely on. Before we ship the redesign, is there anything we should reconsider?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the redesign on its primary user experience improvements without recognizing that the unexpected executive-reporting usage is a signal about a real job (demonstrating engineering reliability and professionalism to stakeholders) that is currently being served by the product — removing the supporting data fields eliminates that job from the product, which will drive churn in a segment whose actual needs were never understood.

problems.prompt: We built a personal finance app that tracks spending, categorizes transactions, and shows budget charts. Engagement is low — users open it once to set up their budget, then barely return. When they do return, it's usually right after a large unexpected expense. Our growth team's plan is to add more gamification (streaks, savings challenges, badges) and push notifications to bring users back daily. What's the core diagnosis and what would a better intervention look like?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates gamification and notification mechanics as engagement drivers, treating this as a retention problem solvable by more app touches, rather than asking what job users actually hire the app for (peace of mind that their finances are under control, not daily entertainment) and recognizing that the 'check after a big expense' usage pattern reveals the real job — the product should minimize the need to actively manage it, not maximize check-ins.

problems.prompt: We're a B2B SaaS that helps sales teams track deals and manage their pipeline. We've been adding AI-powered features: auto-generated email drafts, meeting summaries, and predictive win probabilities. Adoption of the AI features is under 10% even though they're prominently placed. When we talk to sales reps, they say 'I just need to update my manager on where deals stand.' But when we talk to sales managers, they say 'I need to know which deals need my attention right now.' Help us think about why the AI features aren't landing and what the actual job is.
problems.failure_mode: Diagnoses the AI features as a UX problem (prominently placed but underused) and recommends better feature discovery or onboarding, rather than recognizing that the features were designed around an assumed job (help reps do tasks faster) when the actual jobs are distinct for each user type: reps need to reduce friction in reporting up, managers need signal about where to intervene — and the AI features don't serve either job as designed.

problems.prompt: We run a Q&A platform for software developers, similar to Stack Overflow. Our data shows developers spend most of their time reading answers, not writing them. We've been focused on features to encourage more answer-writing: badges, reputation scores, bounties, editor formatting tools. New question volumes and answer volumes are both flat. But traffic is growing because people find answers through Google and read them. What does this pattern tell us about the job our platform is hired for?
problems.failure_mode: Diagnoses flat question/answer volume as a community engagement problem and recommends more incentives for contribution, rather than recognizing the job the platform is actually hired for (find a reliable, trusted answer to a specific coding problem quickly) and that most users are hiring it purely for reading — the product is succeeding at the real job for the majority, and optimizing for contribution metrics may conflict with optimizing for the reading job.

problems.prompt: We make employee onboarding software for mid-size companies. Our product helps HR teams build onboarding workflows, assign tasks to new hires, and track completion. We've been competing aggressively on features with other HR tech vendors. But our churn interviews reveal that companies leave not because of missing features, but because new hires 'still feel lost after 90 days' and managers 'don't know if onboarding is actually working.' We've been adding more workflow features. What should we be building instead?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends more workflow features or task completion reporting as the product direction, staying in the 'HR workflow tool' competitive frame, rather than reframing around the job the buyers actually care about (new employees become productive and stay, and managers can see it's working) which points to features that measure new hire effectiveness and confidence, not task completion rates.

problems.prompt: We're building a feature for our developer tool: an AI assistant that can answer questions about the user's codebase by ingesting their repo. We're planning to invest heavily in making the answers more technically accurate — better code parsing, deeper context windows, smarter embeddings. But we've noticed that the most common follow-up from users after getting an answer is not 'that's wrong' but rather 'how do I do this?' — they take the first answer as a starting point and immediately ask for implementation help. Does this change what we should be building?
problems.failure_mode: Continues prioritizing answer accuracy as the key quality metric, based on the assumption that the job is 'get correct information about my codebase,' rather than recognizing the actual job is 'make progress on a specific implementation task' — and the follow-up pattern reveals that the real competition is having someone to pair-program with, which points the roadmap toward interactive implementation assistance rather than standalone Q&A accuracy.

problems.prompt: We make a project management tool that competes with Asana, Monday, and Jira. We've been asked to evaluate a potential new feature: AI-generated project plans from a simple description. The feature is technically impressive — you describe your project and it creates a full task breakdown with dependencies, durations, and assignments. We've built a prototype. In user testing, people say 'that's cool' and then immediately start editing the generated plan heavily. What should we make of that behavior before we invest in shipping this feature?
problems.failure_mode: Treats 'cool but edited heavily' as a positive signal (they engaged with it) and recommends shipping with better AI quality, rather than investigating what job users are hiring the feature for: the extensive editing reveals that the job isn't 'generate my plan for me' (which the feature does) but 'help me not miss anything and organize my own thinking' (which the current implementation only partly serves) — the real job insight would redirect what to actually build.

problems.prompt: We run a fitness app. We have hundreds of workout programs. Engagement data shows users start an 8-week program, complete weeks 1-2 with high engagement, skip week 3, then open the app less and less. Our growth team's plan is to add more program variety, shorter programs, and more beginner-friendly content. Churn surveys say things like 'I fell off track' and 'I couldn't fit it in.' What's the actual job users are hiring this app for, and how does it change what we should build?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends more program variety and shorter programs as the solution to the engagement cliff, treating this as a content fit problem, rather than identifying the actual job (get back on track reliably when life interrupts, not just stay consistent when things are easy) and recognizing that the 'fell off track' signal means users need friction-free re-entry and progress forgiveness — the real competitive position is 'the app that doesn't punish you for missing a week.'

problems.prompt: We sell a subscription analytics tool to e-commerce companies. Our most engaged users are the founders and CEOs of small e-commerce businesses, who look at the dashboard every morning. We've been building features requested by the data analysts who use the product: custom cohort builders, SQL export, API access for data pipelines. But these features are used by fewer than 5% of our users and haven't moved our retention. The CEO users never asked for any of these. What should we be paying attention to?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the feature backlog based on the analyst requests (our most vocal users) without recognizing the job misalignment: the founders and CEOs are the primary 'hirers' of the product (they decide to keep paying) and their job is 'understand the health of my business first thing every morning so I know what needs my attention' — a completely different job from the analyst's data exploration needs, and one that points to a completely different roadmap.

problems.prompt: We make a code snippet manager — a tool where developers save, tag, and retrieve code snippets they want to reuse. We've been investing in better organization features: nested folders, tag management, search ranking. But usage patterns show that our most active users almost never browse their snippet library. Instead, they search with very specific terms right when they need something. Less active users saved 100+ snippets and then stopped using the tool entirely. What does this tell us about the job the product is hired for?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends better organization features (folders, tagging) based on the assumption that the job is 'build a well-organized personal code library,' without recognizing that the usage data reveals the actual job is 'retrieve a specific thing I know I saved, right now' — and the inactive users are those who believed the job was 'build a library' (which felt like work) while active users hired it for on-demand retrieval, pointing the roadmap toward instant search quality over organization infrastructure.

problems.prompt: We're building a product that helps software engineering teams run retrospectives. Our current features: a timer, anonymous sticky note boards organized by 'what went well / what could improve / action items,' and a voting mechanism. We have 500 teams using it. But most teams only run a retro once and then stop using the product. The exit surveys say 'we didn't see the value after the first one.' We're planning to add more retrospective formats (sailboat, 4Ls, DAKI). Is format variety the right investment?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends more formats as a variety fix for the engagement cliff, framing this as 'teams get bored with one format,' without asking what job the team lead hired the retro tool for (make the team feel heard and produce actions that actually improve things) and recognizing that the 'didn't see value' signal means the action items from the first retro went nowhere — the job isn't being done, not because the format is wrong, but because there's no accountability loop for actions.

problems.prompt: We make a sales enablement platform. One of our most popular features is a library where reps can store and share sales collateral — decks, case studies, competitive battle cards. The library is full (thousands of assets). But sales reps say they still can't find what they need when they're on a call. We've been investing in better search and tagging. Reps are still not satisfied. What should we diagnose from this pattern?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends better search algorithms and metadata management as the solution to the findability problem, treating this as a discoverability engineering problem, rather than examining the real job: reps on a live call need to send the most relevant and persuasive asset for this specific prospect immediately without interrupting the conversation — the job isn't 'search a library' but 'have the right thing appear automatically in context,' which points to proactive surfacing rather than better search.

problems.prompt: We run a learning platform for software developers. We have a vast library of courses — over 2,000. Signups are strong, but 73% of users who start a course don't finish it. We've been investing in shorter courses, better video production quality, and more frequent quizzes to improve completion rates. Completion rates haven't moved. When users are surveyed on why they stopped, the top response is 'I got what I needed.' Is there a reframing that changes what we should build?
problems.failure_mode: Treats 'I got what I needed' as a rationalization for disengagement and recommends better course design to improve completion rates, rather than recognizing this as a signal about the actual job: developers hire courses not to 'complete a curriculum' but to 'solve a specific immediate problem or fill a specific skill gap' — the 'course completion' metric measures the wrong job, and the product should optimize for 'did they get what they came for quickly' rather than driving completion of a longer path.

problems.prompt: We built an AI-powered meeting notes tool that integrates with calendar and Zoom. It automatically joins meetings, records them, generates a summary, and sends it to all attendees. Adoption is 40%, which is decent, but we've received complaints from several enterprise customers saying 'employees feel surveilled' and 'the summaries create a paper trail people are uncomfortable with.' We're thinking of adding privacy controls. But before we do, what's the underlying job mismatch worth examining?
problems.failure_mode: Treats the surveillance concern as a configuration problem (add opt-out, privacy controls) and recommends technical privacy features, without examining the job mismatch: the tool was designed to serve the organizer's job (reduce note-taking overhead, have a record) while attendees have a different and sometimes conflicting job (speak freely and not create an immutable record) — the product conflict isn't fixable with controls if the fundamental jobs of organizer and attendee are structurally in tension.

problems.prompt: We have a B2B product with a free community tier and a paid enterprise tier. Conversion from community to enterprise is 2%. When we look at who converts, it's almost exclusively teams that have grown to 15+ users on the free tier and are hitting seat limits. Teams of 2-5 almost never convert. We've been building features that appeal to small teams (better UI, more integrations) hoping to improve small-team conversion. Should we keep investing in small-team features?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends continued investment in small-team features as the conversion lever, based on the assumption that small teams aren't converting because of missing features, rather than asking what job drives the enterprise upgrade decision (I need to govern, administer, and secure this tool across my org — a job that only becomes real at a certain team size) and recognizing that no small-team feature will create that job if it doesn't exist yet at their scale.

problems.prompt: We run a social reading app where users share book highlights and notes. We've been trying to grow engagement by making the social feed more prominent and adding features like reactions, book clubs, and reading challenges. Engagement with the social features is low. When we look at the most-retained users, they're people who use the app entirely privately — they save highlights, write notes, and never share or follow anyone. What does this tell us about the job the product is actually hired for?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends improving the social features to fix social engagement, treating this as a product quality problem for social functionality, rather than recognizing that the retention data reveals the dominant job is 'capture and retrieve insights from what I read, for my own use' — a fundamentally private utility job that the social layer is not just irrelevant to, but potentially competing with by creating social performance pressure.

problems.prompt: We make a time-tracking tool for freelancers. Our most common feature request from churned users is 'I wish it could automatically track my time without me having to start a timer.' We've been investing in automatic time-tracking via calendar and app activity. But when we built an early version and gave it to users, most disabled it after a week. They said 'the automated tracking wasn't accurate enough.' What's the real job here and what should we actually build?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends making the automatic tracking more accurate as the technical fix to the stated feature request, treating this as a precision problem, rather than asking what job 'automatic time tracking' was hired for: freelancers asking for automatic tracking are trying to solve the job of 'minimize the overhead of proving to clients that I worked the hours I invoiced' — the accuracy problem may be unsolvable, but the actual job might be served by a completely different approach (lightweight logging, client-visible summaries) that doesn't require perfect automatic tracking.

problems.prompt: We've built a developer changelog tool — when a team ships a feature, they write a release note in our tool, which publishes to a public page. We thought developers would love transparent public changelogs for their APIs. Adoption among teams is low, and the changelogs that do get published have sparse content ('fixed bug in endpoint X'). The teams that use it seem to use it reactively — only after a customer complains about something changing. What's the job mismatch here?
problems.failure_mode: Diagnoses the quality problem as a writing experience issue (make it easier to write good changelogs) and recommends templates and prompts, rather than examining who the tool is actually hired by and for what job: the developers building APIs weren't looking for a tool to communicate with customers — they were surprised to discover customers need communication when things change; the job belongs to a different person (customer success, developer relations) and the tool is aimed at the wrong job performer.

problems.prompt: We built a knowledge base tool for engineering teams — a wiki-like system where teams document runbooks, architecture decisions, and best practices. After 6 months, the usage pattern is odd: teams create documents eagerly in the first month, then stop creating new ones, but continue searching and reading. Several teams have requested 'AI-powered answers from our docs.' Before building that feature, help me think about what job the knowledge base is actually being hired for.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the AI-answers feature request at face value as an enhancement to the existing document Q&A use case, rather than investigating the job the tool is hired for: the pattern (burst of creation, then search-only) suggests the job being served is 'find the answer to a problem I'm having right now' rather than 'maintain living documentation' — and AI-answers would serve the retrieval job better, but it's worth understanding whether the value proposition is 'documentation tool' or 'knowledge retrieval tool' before investing.

problems.prompt: We built a product that lets users create beautiful visual presentations from data — connecting to data sources and auto-generating charts with AI-suggested narratives. We thought the job was 'make great data presentations faster.' But our power users almost exclusively use it for one specific workflow: their weekly executive business review. They build the same report each week with updated data. Occasional users who tried it for one-off presentations churned. What does this usage pattern tell us about the job, and how should it change our roadmap?
problems.failure_mode: Treats the weekly recurring use as one instance of the general 'create data presentations' job and recommends broader improvements to serve all presentation use cases, rather than recognizing that the actual job being reliably hired for is 'reduce the painful weekly ritual of updating and sending the executive report' — a specific recurring job with specific pain points (updating the same visuals, making it look consistent, getting it done before Monday's meeting) that would point to automation, templating, and scheduled delivery rather than general presentation capability.
